title: Warmup timing vulnerability: stake() inflates epoch.distribute by calling rebase() before updating gonsInWarmup
claim: In the stake() function, FLOOR tokens are transferred to the staking contract before rebase() is called, but gonsInWarmup is updated after rebase() returns. During rebase(), the profit calculation `epoch.distribute = FLOOR.balanceOf(this) - sFLOOR.circulatingSupply()` uses a balance that includes the new deposit while circulatingSupply excludes it (because gonsInWarmup hasn't been updated). This inflates epoch.distribute by the deposit amount, causing the deposit to be treated as protocol profit and distributed to all sFLOOR holders in the next rebase. The attacker can claim their warmup position immediately (when warmupPeriod=0 with _claim=false, expiry equals current epoch.number) and profit from the inflated distribution.
impact: An attacker can extract value from other stakers by having their deposit counted as profit. When the protocol is solvent (FLOOR balance >= circulatingSupply), the ROI ranges from 20% (when depositing 25% of circulating supply) to 50% (when matching circulating supply). The attack dilutes all existing sFLOOR holders and can be repeated. The vulnerability is triggerable even with warmupPeriod=0 by calling stake() with _claim=false, which routes through the warmup path and allows immediate claiming due to expiry = epoch.number.
paths: 1. Attacker calls stake(attacker, amount, true, false) - FLOOR transferred to staking contract
2. Inside stake(), rebase() is called: balance includes the new deposit, staked excludes it (gonsInWarmup not yet updated), epoch.distribute = balance - staked is inflated by deposit amount
3. Warmup entry created with expiry = epoch.number (since warmupPeriod=0 or after increment)
4. Attacker calls claim(attacker, true) immediately - receives sFLOOR worth their deposit
5. Attacker (or anyone) calls rebase() - distributes inflated epoch.distribute to all sFLOOR holders including attacker
6. Attacker calls unstake() to convert sFLOOR back to FLOOR for profit
Optimal parameters (when protocol solvent): deposit = circulatingSupply (e.g., 947,420 FLOOR) gives 50% ROI. With the FlawVerifier cap of circulatingSupply/4 (236,855 FLOOR): 20% ROI

title: Protocol insolvency: circulating supply exceeds FLOOR backing by ~21%
claim: On-chain data shows sFLOOR.circulatingSupply() = 947,419.87 FLOOR while FLOOR.balanceOf(staking) = 748,917.81 FLOOR, creating a shortfall of ~198,502 FLOOR (~21% of circulating supply). The unstake() function requires `amount_ <= FLOOR.balanceOf(address(this))`, meaning only ~79% of sFLOOR/gFLOOR claims can be redeemed. The remaining ~21% of holders cannot unstake and will lose their funds. This insolvency creates a bank-run dynamic where the first users to unstake are made whole while later users are left with unredeemable tokens.
impact: Approximately 21% of sFLOOR/gFLOOR value is unbacked and cannot be redeemed. Users who unstake later will find their transactions reverting with 'Insufficient FLOOR balance in contract'. This represents a systemic protocol failure where ~198,502 FLOOR worth of claims are permanently impaired. The insolvency may have resulted from past exploitation of the warmup timing vulnerability (F-001) or from the rebase profit calculation not properly accounting for all liabilities.
paths: 1. Any sFLOOR/gFLOOR holder attempts to unstake via unstake()
2. The require(amount_ <= FLOOR.balanceOf(address(this))) check passes for early unstakers
3. After ~748,918 FLOOR has been withdrawn, subsequent unstake attempts revert
4. Remaining holders (~21% of circulating supply) cannot redeem their tokens
Current on-chain: 947,419 FLOOR circulating, 748,918 FLOOR backing, 198,502 FLOOR shortfall
